---
title: "Lecture 1: Basics"
subtitle: "Course objectives, schedule, assignments · Introduction to R · Live coding"
topics:
  - Course objectives, schedule and assignments
  - Introduction to R and RStudio
  - "Live coding: variables, vectors, matrices, data frames, lists, functions, loops"
  - Data import and export
---

## Welcome to the course {.section-divider}

::: {.callout-note title="Today's roadmap"}
1. **Course logistics** — objectives, schedule, grading, registration.
2. **Why R?** — and how RStudio fits in.
3. **Live coding** — the building blocks of R.
4. **Wrap-up** — what to prepare before Lecture 2.
:::

::: notes
Open with the survey and a short round of intros: who has used R, who has used Python, who has never coded.
:::

# 1.1 Course objectives, schedule, and assignments {.section-divider}

## Welcome

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Prof. Dr. Andre Guettler** · Director of the Institute
Helmholtzstraße 22, Room 205
[andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de](mailto:andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de)
+49 731 50 31 030

**Oliver Padmaperuma** · Doctoral Candidate
Helmholtzstraße 22, Room 203
[oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de)
+49 731 50 31 036
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
- **Registration:** register for "exam" **{{< meta exam-id >}}** in `{{< meta registration-system >}}` by **{{< meta registration-deadline >}}**. Without registration you cannot submit problem sets or the review.
- Registered students who don't submit receive a fail grade (5.0).
- Questions are best asked **during or immediately after** class.
- Administrative matters → [studiensekretariat@uni-ulm.de](mailto:studiensekretariat@uni-ulm.de).
:::

:::

## Course objective

| Objective | Approach | Assignments |
|---|---|---|
| Equip students with the practical toolkit and conceptual grounding for empirical finance research | Four lectures + Brown Bag Seminar; weekly live coding in R; LaTeX/Overleaf for write-up | 50% group problem set (R + LaTeX); 50% individual referee report |

## Schedule

| | Lecture 1 | Lecture 2 | Lecture 3 | Lecture 4 | Brown Bag Seminar |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Date** | {{< meta lecture-01-basics-date-short >}} | {{< meta lecture-02-data-handling-date-short >}} | {{< meta lecture-03-statistical-analysis-date-short >}} | {{< meta lecture-04-academic-publishing-date-short >}} | {{< meta lecture-05-brown-bag-seminar-date-short >}} |
| **Theme** | Course intro & R fundamentals | R Data Handling & Visualization | Statistical Analysis using R | Academic Publishing process | Apply tips to a doctoral talk |

## Assignment I — Problem Set (50%)

- Solve the problem set posted on Moodle, building on Lectures 1–3.
- Submit **two files** as a group of up to **5** students:
  1. one well-commented `.R` script (efficient, meaningful variable names, no needless loops);
  2. one PDF compiled from the Overleaf template (plots, captions, LaTeX tables, written text).
- **Deadline:** {{< meta assignment-1-problem-set-deadline >}} (midnight). Email to [oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de), CC [andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de](mailto:andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de).
- File / subject pattern: `RiF_ProblemSet_surname1_surname2_...`

## Assignment II — Referee Report (50%)

- **Attend the mandatory Brown Bag Seminar on {{< meta lecture-05-brown-bag-seminar-date >}}, {{< meta lecture-05-brown-bag-seminar-time >}}.**
- Pick one doctoral presentation, apply the writing/publishing/refereeing tips from the course.
- Submit one 2.5–3 page PDF report (academic referee style) per group of up to **5**.
- **Deadline:** {{< meta assignment-2-referee-report-deadline >}}. Email to [oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de](mailto:oliver.padmaperuma@uni-ulm.de), CC [andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de](mailto:andre.guettler@uni-ulm.de).

::: {.callout-tip title="Any questions before we begin?"}
Now is the moment.
:::

# 1.2 Introduction to R {.section-divider}

## Survey — let's get to know you

::: notes
Mentimeter survey: study program, prior coding proficiency. Use the results to calibrate pace.
:::

> Open the Mentimeter link shared in class.

## Why learn R?

- It's **free and open-source**.
- A powerful tool for **data analysis and graphics**.
- R code is great for **reproducibility and shareability** — submit your script and anyone can re-run your results.
- A **large, active community** continuously extends R via **packages**.

## What are R and RStudio?

- **R is a programming language** for statistical computing, data analytics, and scientific research. It is one of the most widely used languages by statisticians and researchers to manage, manipulate, analyze, and visualize data.
- **RStudio** is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R that makes interaction with R easier — code completion, debugging, project management, plot viewing.
- In 2022 RStudio was rebranded **Posit** to signal its move toward language-agnostic tooling.
- **You must install R first**, then RStudio.

## Installing R and RStudio

::: incremental
1. Go to <https://cran.r-project.org/> and click **Download R for Windows / macOS**.
2. Click **base** subdirectory.
3. Click **Download R-x.x.x for Windows** and run the `.exe`. Accept defaults.
4. Go to <https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop> and click **Download RStudio**.
5. Run the RStudio installer.
:::

## RStudio interface

Three main panes when you open RStudio:

1. **Console** — where R code is executed.
2. **Global Environment / History** — your in-memory objects.
3. **Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer** — the swiss-army-knife pane.

<!-- TODO: figure from PDF p.16 — RStudio interface screenshot showing the three panes; recreate or import as image into images/ -->

## Writing code in RStudio

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Script Editor
- Load and save `.R` scripts.
- Keeps a record of your analysis — show others how it was run, repeat later.
- Edit code without accidentally running it.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Console
- Where R code is **executed**.
- You can also run code interactively here, but it is **NOT** saved on disk.
- See output and errors directly.
:::

:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Best practice"}
Write and run code through the **Script Editor**. Use the Console only for quick exploration.
:::

## Setting the working directory

The **working directory** (`wd`) is where R looks for and saves files.

Two ways to set it:

```r
# Option 1: menu
# Session → Set Working Directory → Choose Directory…

# Option 2: code
setwd("C:/Users/<you>/Documents/research-in-finance")

# Verify
getwd()
```

## Getting started — running code

The blinking cursor in the Script Editor prompts you to write. The Console shows `>` when R is ready.

Four ways to run code:

1. **Ctrl/Cmd + Enter** — run current line / selection.
2. Click **Run**.
3. **Ctrl + Shift + Enter** — run all lines in the editor.
4. Highlight lines and click **Run**.

::: {.callout-tip title="Practice"}
Type `1 + 2*8` and `log(10)`. Result appears in the Console with a leading `[1]` (the index of the first element on that line).
:::

## What are packages?

- Packages **expand** what you can do beyond base R.
- A collection of **functions, data sets, and other R objects** under one name.
- Install from repositories: **CRAN**, GitHub, etc.
- The **Packages** tab lists installed packages; click **Update** to upgrade; the small × uninstalls.

## Installing and loading packages

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Installing
```r
# From CRAN (default)
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("tidyquant")
install.packages("Quandl")
```
Or click **Install** in the Packages tab.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Loading
```r
library(tidyverse)
library(tidyquant)
library(Quandl)
```
Or tick the package's checkbox in the Packages tab.
:::

:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Short exercise"}
Install and load the `tidyverse`, `tidyquant`, and `Quandl` packages.
:::

# 1.3 Live Coding Session 1 {.section-divider}

## Creating objects / variables

- Assign with `<-` (the assignment operator).

::: incremental
- ❌ avoid long object names · ✅ be descriptive
- ❌ don't reuse names of existing R functions
- ❌ no blank spaces — use `_` or `.`
:::

```r
# Practice
x <- 5
x + x   # → [1] 10
```

## Four data types in R

1. **Numeric** — numbers that may contain decimals.
   - **Integer** — special case, no decimals (suffix `L`).
2. **Character** — text (strings); wrap in `" "`.
3. **Factor** — special character used for **categorical data** (e.g., male/female, months).
4. **Logical** — Boolean (`TRUE` / `FALSE`).

::: {.callout-tip title="Class lookup"}
`class(objectname)` returns the higher-level label R uses to decide which functions and behaviors apply (`"data.frame"`, `"factor"`, `"matrix"`, `"numeric"`).
:::

## Variables and basic operations

```r
myNumber  <- 3                                   # numeric (default)
myInteger <- 10L                                 # integer ('L' suffix)
myText    <- "Some sentence..."                  # character
myFactor  <- factor(c("red", "blue", "red", "green"))  # factor
levels(myFactor)                                 # "blue" "green" "red"
myLogical <- TRUE                                # logical
```

> **Why:** assign and compute financial metrics — returns, prices, ratios — quickly during empirical work.

## Five basic data structures

1. **Individual values** — single scalars.
2. **Vectors** — ordered collections of elements (one type only).
3. **Matrices** — 2D arrays; rows × columns; one type only.
4. **Data frames** — like spreadsheets; columns can have **different types**.
5. **Lists** — flexible containers that can hold vectors, matrices, data frames, even other lists.

## Vectors

```r
# Create
myVector <- c(3, 4, -1.1, pi)

# Inspect / aggregate
myVector[3]        # third element: -1.1
length(myVector)   # 4
sum(myVector)      # ~9.04
mean(myVector)     # ~2.26
var(myVector)      # ~5.21
sort(myVector)     # ascending: -1.1, 3.0, 3.14, 4.0
quantile(myVector, c(0.1, 0.25, 0.95))

# Vectorized arithmetic — no loops needed
myVector + 2
myVector * myVector

# Coercion: mixed types collapse to character
mixed <- c(1, "two", TRUE)   # "1" "two" "TRUE"

# Named vectors
namedVec <- c(apple = 5, banana = 3)
namedVec["banana"]   # 3
```

## Matrices

```r
myMatrix <- matrix(c(2, 1, 0, -9, 5, 0), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
dim(myMatrix)              # [1] 2 3
myMatrix[1, 1] <- 2.1415   # modify cell
myMatrix[, 2]              # second column

# Linear algebra
anotherMatrix <- matrix(c(5, -1, 7, 0, 2, -1), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)
transposedMatrix <- t(anotherMatrix)
matrixProduct    <- myMatrix %*% transposedMatrix     # 2x2 product
matrixInverse    <- solve(matrixProduct)              # inverse

# Aggregations
rowSums(myMatrix)
colMeans(myMatrix)

# Bind: combine rows / cols
cbind(myMatrix, c(6, 7))
```

> **Use case:** covariance matrices for portfolio optimization or regressions.

## Data frames

```r
student_name <- c("Student A", "Student B", "Student C")
grade        <- c(1.7, 2.3, 5.0)
students     <- data.frame(student_name, grade)

students$grade                    # column access
colnames(students)[2] <- "exam2021"
students[2:3, ]                   # rows 2-3

# Add columns
students$degree    <- c("Bachelor", "Master", "Bachelor")
students$pass_fail <- c("Pass", "Pass", "Fail")
table(students$degree, students$pass_fail)

# Summary
summary(students)

# Subset by condition
subset(students, exam2021 > 2)

# Add row
newStudent <- data.frame(student_name = "Student D", exam2021 = 3.5,
                         degree = "PhD", pass_fail = "Pass")
students <- rbind(students, newStudent)

# Merge with another frame
ages   <- data.frame(student_name = c("Student A", "Student B", "Student C"),
                     age = c(20, 22, 21))
merged <- merge(students, ages, by = "student_name")
```

> **Use case:** organizing prices/dates for thesis-level analysis and merging multiple sources.

## Lists

```r
scoresList <- list(
  scores = c(95, 85, 92),
  names  = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol"),
  passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

str(scoresList)

scoresList$scores[3] <- 90               # update Carol's score
scoresList$comments  <- "All passed"     # add element

# Nested list
nestedList <- list(course = "Math 101", details = scoresList)
nestedList$details$names[1]              # "Alice"

# Convert to data frame (elements need equal length)
scoresDF <- as.data.frame(scoresList)
```

## Functions

```r
# Define
bmiCalc <- function(height, weight) {
  bmi <- weight / (height ^ 2)
  return(round(bmi, 1))
}

# Call
myBmi <- bmiCalc(1.75, 70)   # 22.9

# Default arguments
bmiCalcDefault <- function(height, weight = 70) {
  round(weight / (height ^ 2), 1)
}
bmiCalcDefault(1.75)         # 22.9

# Multiple returns via list
bmiAdvanced <- function(height, weight) {
  bmi <- weight / (height ^ 2)
  category <- ifelse(bmi < 18.5, "Underweight",
                     ifelse(bmi < 25, "Normal", "Overweight"))
  list(bmi = round(bmi, 1), category = category)
}
result <- bmiAdvanced(1.75, 70)
result$bmi        # 22.9
result$category   # "Normal"
```

> **Use case:** automate repetitive calculations (risk-adjusted returns, simulation models) for efficiency in large datasets.

## Loops

```r
nums <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

# For-loop: sum even numbers
evenSum <- 0
for (i in nums) {
  if (i %% 2 == 0) evenSum <- evenSum + i
}
evenSum    # 6

# While-loop
j <- 1
while (j <= 3) {
  print(j)
  j <- j + 1
}

# Nested for-loop
for (a in 1:2) {
  for (b in 3:4) {
    print(a * b)   # 3, 4, 6, 8
  }
}
```

::: {.callout-tip title="Pro tip"}
For data-frame operations, **vectorized** alternatives (e.g., `apply`, `dplyr::mutate`) are usually faster and clearer than for-loops.
:::

## Data import & export — file types

You will rarely build matrices/data frames by hand. Common ways to **read** data:

- **Text** — `.TXT` (`readLines()`)
- **Tabular** — `.CSV`, `.TSV` (`read.table()`, `readr::read_csv()`)
- **Excel** — `.XLSX` (`xlsx`, `readxl`)
- **Google sheets** — `googlesheets4`
- **Statistics programs** — SPSS, SAS (`haven`)
- **Databases** — MySQL (`RMySQL`)

## Data import & export — examples

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Import
```r
# CSV
objectname <- read.csv("your_file.csv", header = TRUE)

# Tab-delimited
objectname <- read.table("your_file.txt", sep = "\t", header = TRUE)

# Excel
library(readxl)
objectname <- read_excel("your_file.xlsx")

# RDS (R single-object binary)
objectname <- readRDS("your_file.rds")
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Export
```r
# CSV
write.csv(objectname, file = "output_file.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# Tab-delimited
write.table(objectname, file = "output_file.txt",
            sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, col.names = TRUE)

# Excel
library(writexl)
write_xlsx(objectname, "output_file.xlsx")

# RDS
saveRDS(objectname, file = "output_file.rds")
```
:::

:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Separators"}
The argument `sep =` may not be necessary — R recognises `,` in `.csv` and whitespace in `.txt`. But sometimes R won't read correctly unless you specify it.
:::

## Saving your script

1. Save every step in an `.R` script. Comment lines start with `#` and are ignored at run time.
2. **File → Save**, or click the disk icon, or **Ctrl/Cmd + S**.
3. Unsaved scripts (or unsaved edits) show **red text and an asterisk** in the tab name.

## Closing an R session

Three ways to close RStudio:

1. **File → Quit Session**
2. The red ✕ in the top-right corner.
3. Run `q()` in the Console.

::: {.callout-tip title="Don't save the workspace image (.RData)"}
Unless (a) you ran something very expensive, or (b) you're nearly done with the project. Starting clean each session avoids hidden state from previous runs.
:::

# 1.4 Conclusion of Lecture 1 {.section-divider}

## Topics covered today

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
- Basic R Functionality
- Variables & Basic Operations
- Vectors
- Matrices
- Data Frames
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- Lists
- Functions
- Loops
- Data Import & Export
:::

:::

## Further reading

- James, G., Witten, D., Hastie, T., & Tibshirani, R. (2021). *An Introduction to Statistical Learning with applications in R* (2nd ed.). Springer. Free PDF: <https://www.statlearning.com/>
- Scheuch, C., Voigt, S., & Weiss, P. (2023). *Tidy Finance with R*. Chapman & Hall/CRC. Free: <https://www.tidy-finance.org/r/>
- Wickham, H., & Çetinkaya-Rundel, M. (2023). *R for Data Science* (2nd ed.). O'Reilly. Free: <https://r4ds.hadley.nz/>
- Kaggle Discussions (2021). *List of free R books*. <https://www.kaggle.com/discussions/general/274029>

## Prepare before next lecture

1. **Document today's code** in a clean way and save as `.Rmd`.
2. **Set up the Nasdaq Data Link API:**
   1. Register a free account at <https://data.nasdaq.com/> (formerly Quandl).
   2. Log in, go to your profile, **copy your personal API key**.
   3. *Optional challenge:* load the `Quandl` library, set your key with `Quandl.api_key("your_key_here")`, and test loading data. We'll walk through this in Lecture 2.

## See you next time

::: {.callout-note title="Reminder"}
- **Register** for "exam" {{< meta exam-id >}} in `{{< meta registration-system >}}` by **{{< meta registration-deadline >}}**.
- Bring your laptop with R + RStudio installed and the `tidyverse`, `tidyquant`, `Quandl` packages already loaded.
:::

> **Lecture 2:** R Data Handling and Visualization — APIs, merging, cleansing, `dplyr`, `ggplot2`.
